required.title: [For Student] A physical planner or planning notebook

suggested.title: [For Student] My Side of the Mountain
suggested.author: Jean Craighead George

competence_markers.description: The student builds their own daily plan — listing tasks, estimating times, sequencing, and time-blocking — without adult assistance on most days
Time estimates are calibrating: the gap between estimated and actual duration is narrowing over weeks and months
The student meets internal deadlines they set for themselves, not just external ones imposed by others
The student manages multiple concurrent demands — academic work, chores, training, commitments — within the same day without one category consistently being abandoned
When the plan breaks, the student recovers constructively — replanning the remainder rather than abandoning the day
The student conducts a meaningful weekly review, with specific observations about completion rates, estimation accuracy, and energy patterns
Time-blocking is practiced regularly and the student can articulate why it matters for their productivity
The student demonstrates growing accuracy in the time log — where they think their time goes and where it actually goes are converging
Responsibility handoffs from mentor to student are maintained reliably — what is transferred stays transferred

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through how you plan a day.
narration_prompts.detail: What is your process? What tools do you use? Where do you start? How do you decide what order things go in?

narration_prompts.prompt: How accurate are your time estimates?
narration_prompts.detail: Where are you consistently off? What do you do about it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a time your plan broke.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What did you do? Looking back, what would you do differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between a to-do list and a time-blocked plan?
narration_prompts.detail: Which do you use? Why does it matter?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is your biggest time management weakness right now?
narration_prompts.detail: Be honest. What are you doing about it?
What do you look at? What have you learned from it? Give a specific example of something you changed based on review data.
What happens in your day regardless of what else breaks or changes?

cross_connections.description: The character companion. IC1.1-B develops the will to own your time and keep commitments. IC2.1-B develops the practical system. Character without system is well-meaning but disorganized. System without character is a plan you will not follow when it gets hard.

Time management without quality standards produces a lot of mediocre work done on schedule. IC2.2 provides the quality filter: it is not enough that the work is done on time — it must be done well. Time-blocking must account for doing work at standard, not just doing it.

Time-blocking creates the conditions for deep work. A protected time block with a specific task is the architecture that makes sustained focus possible. IC2.3 builds the internal capacity for focus; IC2.1-B builds the external structure that protects it.

Daily planning is the atomic unit of long-term project execution. The multi-week and multi-month projects in IC2.4 are accomplished through daily plans that connect to weekly plans that connect to the project timeline. IC2.1-B provides the execution engine.

Schedule recovery is resilience training in its most practical form. The student who learns to recover from a broken day without emotional collapse is building the same capacity that IC3 develops for larger disruptions.

Time is the first capital you manage. The planning, estimation, and review disciplines you build here transfer directly to financial planning. The student who cannot manage time will struggle to manage money — both require honest estimation, delayed gratification, and consistent review.

The primary modern threat to time architecture is digital distraction. As you build your time-blocking practice, you will discover that phones, notifications, and screens are the most common reason plans break. IC2.1-B establishes the time structure; 4.2.6 addresses the digital threats to it.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you lived inside a rhythm someone else built. You learned to follow it, own it, and eventually run it without being reminded. That was the right foundation — you needed external structure before you could create your own.

Now the structure becomes yours to design.

Weathering Storms shifts the question from "Can you follow the daily rhythm?" to "Can you build your own?" From "Can you do your chores at the right time?" to "Can you plan an entire day — deciding what needs to happen, in what order, for how long — and then execute that plan?"

This is one of the most important transitions in human development, and most people never fully make it. Look around at the adults in the world. Many of them cannot plan their time. They chronically underestimate how long things take. They start the day without a plan and wonder where the hours went. They miss deadlines, forget commitments, and operate in a permanent state of reacting to whatever demand arrives next. These are not lazy people. They are people who never learned what this packet teaches: **how to own time rather than be owned by it.**

You are going to learn to plan your days, estimate how long things actually take, manage multiple demands, meet deadlines you set for yourself, and recover when your plan falls apart — because it will fall apart. Plans always do. The question is whether you can rebuild and keep going, or whether one broken day becomes a broken week.

This is not theory. It is daily practice. You will plan tomorrow today, execute the plan, review what actually happened, and use the data to plan better the next day. Over months, your plans will become realistic, your execution will become reliable, and you will develop something most people never have: an honest, tested understanding of what you can actually accomplish in a day.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Daily planning** — building your own schedule each day, from scratch, based on what actually needs to happen
2. **Time estimation** — accurately predicting how long tasks will take, calibrated by real data rather than optimism
3. **Meeting deadlines** — internal deadlines you set for yourself, not just external ones imposed by others
4. **Managing multiple demands** — academic work, chores, training, commitments, and personal projects, all in the same day
5. **Schedule recovery** — getting back on track when the plan breaks, without abandoning the entire day or week
6. **Weekly and daily review** — honest assessment of what you planned vs. what you accomplished, used as data for improvement
7. **Time-blocking** — assigning specific work to specific windows, not leaving tasks as vague intentions
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Planning Fallacy — Why Your Estimates Are Wrong

Here is something you will discover almost immediately when you start planning your own days: **everything takes longer than you think it will.**

This is not because you are bad at planning. It is because you are human. Psychologists call this the **planning fallacy** — the consistent tendency to underestimate how long tasks will take. It is one of the most well-documented cognitive biases in psychology. Adults suffer from it. Professionals suffer from it. Construction projects, software development, writing deadlines — the planning fallacy affects everyone, everywhere.

Why does it happen? Because when you estimate how long something will take, you imagine the best-case version: no interruptions, no confusion, no mistakes, no slow patches. You plan for the version of yourself that works at maximum speed with zero friction. That version does not exist.

**The cure is data.** Not guesses. Not feelings. Data.

**Your first exercise:** For one full week, before you start any task — schoolwork, chores, practice, a project — write down how long you think it will take. Then time yourself. Actually time yourself, with a clock or a timer. When the task is done, write down how long it actually took. At the end of the week, compare.

You will almost certainly find:
- Most things took longer than you estimated
- Some things took much longer
- A few things took less time (usually the ones you were dreading)
- The gap between your estimates and reality is significant

This gap is the planning fallacy. The only way to close it is to keep measuring. Over weeks and months of tracking estimates against actuals, your estimates will calibrate. You will develop something rare and valuable: an accurate sense of how long things actually take.

### Part 2: Daily Planning — You Build Tomorrow Today

Starting now, you plan your own day. Not your mentor. Not your parent. You.

**The daily planning process:**

**Step 1: List everything that needs to happen.** The evening before or first thing in the morning, write down every task for the coming day. Not categories — specific tasks.

Wrong: "Do schoolwork."
Right: "Read Chapter 7 of history (30 min). Complete math problems 1-25 (45 min). Write narration on science reading (20 min). Practice piano (30 min)."

Wrong: "Do chores."
Right: "Feed animals (15 min). Clean kitchen after lunch (20 min). Take out trash (5 min)."

**Step 2: Estimate the time for each task.** Write your estimate next to each task. You will be wrong at first — that is the point. You are calibrating.

**Step 3: Sequence the tasks.** Decide what order makes sense. Some things have fixed times (meals, appointments, lessons). The rest you arrange. General principles:
- Do the hardest cognitive work when your energy is highest (usually morning)
- Do not save demanding work for the end of the day when you are tired
- Group similar tasks together when possible
- Build in transition time — you cannot end one task and immediately begin another with zero gap

**Step 4: Time-block.** Assign each task to a specific time window. Not "sometime today" — a specific window. "Math: 9:00-9:45. History reading: 10:00-10:30." This is time-blocking, and it transforms vague intentions into concrete commitments. A task assigned to a specific time is dramatically more likely to happen than a task floating on a list.

**Step 5: Execute.** Work through your plan. When a task is done, check it off. When you are in a time-block, that task has your full attention — not half-attention while thinking about the next thing.

**Step 6: Review.** At the end of the day, spend five minutes reviewing. What did you finish? What did you not finish? Why? Was the plan realistic? Were your time estimates accurate? What would you change?

This six-step process is your daily discipline. It is not optional. It is not something you do when you feel like it. It is the system by which you run your day — every day.

### Part 3: Time Estimation — Building an Accurate Internal Clock

The ability to estimate how long things take is not a talent. It is a skill, built through practice and data.

**The calibration process:**
1. Before each task, write your estimate
2. Time the actual duration
3. Record the difference
4. Over time, patterns emerge: you consistently underestimate reading. You overestimate cleanup. Math takes exactly as long as you think. Writing takes twice as long.
5. Adjust your future estimates based on real data

**Common estimation errors:**
- **Ignoring setup and transition time.** "Piano practice: 30 minutes" might actually be 40 minutes when you include getting your music out, warming up, and putting everything away.
- **Assuming no interruptions.** Every day has interruptions. Build a buffer — 10-15% extra time in your plan for the things you cannot predict.
- **Estimating based on the easy version.** Your math estimate should include the hard problems, not just the ones you can do quickly.
- **Forgetting recurring tasks.** Meals, hygiene, chores, transitions — these consume hours of every day. If they are not in your plan, your plan is a fantasy.

**A practical tool: The Time Log.** For one week each month, keep a detailed log of how you actually spend your time. Every 30 minutes, write down what you did. At the end of the week, add it up. Where did the time actually go? Most people are shocked by the gap between where they think their time goes and where it actually goes. The time log eliminates that gap.

### Part 4: Managing Multiple Demands

Your life has multiple categories of work, and they all need time:
- Academic work (reading, writing, math, science, history)
- Physical training
- Chores and household responsibilities
- Practice and skill development (music, craft, language)
- Personal projects
- Rest and recovery
- Social commitments

All of these must fit inside the same 24 hours. They compete for your time. If you do not plan deliberately, the urgent things will consume the important things, the easy things will displace the hard things, and the things you enjoy will crowd out the things you need.

**The priority framework:**
1. **Non-negotiables first.** These are the things that happen regardless: sleep, meals, hygiene, chores. They go into the plan before anything else because they are not optional.
2. **Important and demanding second.** Your hardest academic work, your most challenging practice — the things that require your best energy. Schedule them during your peak hours, not in the leftover gaps.
3. **Important but routine third.** Tasks that need to happen but do not require peak cognitive effort. These fill the middle of the day.
4. **Flexible and optional last.** Free time, personal projects, leisure. These are real and important — rest is not optional — but they fit around the priorities, not the other way around.

**The key insight:** If you plan your day by doing whatever feels most appealing first, the hard and important work will get pushed to the end, where you have the least energy, and it will be done poorly or not at all. The person who runs their day by feeling is always behind. The person who runs their day by plan is consistently ahead.

### Part 5: Meeting Deadlines — Especially Your Own

External deadlines are easy. Someone else sets them, and there are consequences for missing them. Internal deadlines — the ones you set for yourself — are harder, because the only person who knows you missed them is you.

IC2.1-B develops the habit of setting and keeping internal deadlines.

**How to set real internal deadlines:**
- State the deadline specifically: "I will finish this chapter by Thursday at 3:00 PM." Not "I'll try to finish it this week."
- Write the deadline in your planner. A deadline that exists only in your head is not a real deadline.
- Work backward from the deadline to determine the daily work required. If the chapter is due Thursday and it takes four hours to read and narrate, you need to start Monday.
- When the deadline arrives, assess honestly: did you meet it? If yes, note what worked. If no, diagnose why — was the estimate wrong? did you fail to execute? did something unexpected interfere?

**Why internal deadlines matter:** They are practice for the real world, where the most important deadlines are the ones no one else sets for you. The person who can only meet external deadlines — tests, assignments, appointments imposed by others — is dependent on external structure. The person who meets self-imposed deadlines has internalized the discipline. That person does not need a boss standing over them. They are their own boss.

### Part 6: Recovering from Broken Schedules

Your plan will break. Not sometimes. Regularly. A task takes twice as long as estimated. Someone gets sick. An appointment runs over. The weather changes the outdoor plan. Something comes up.

**A broken plan is not a failed day.** A broken plan is information. The question is not "did the plan hold perfectly?" but "what did I do when it broke?"

**The recovery process:**
1. **Acknowledge the break.** Something did not happen as planned. That is a fact, not a catastrophe.
2. **Assess what remains.** What still needs to happen today? What can move to tomorrow? What is truly urgent versus what merely feels urgent?
3. **Rebuild the remainder.** Replan the rest of the day with the time you actually have. This might mean some tasks get shortened, some get moved, and some get dropped. Make deliberate choices about what stays and what moves — do not just let the rest of the day happen randomly.
4. **Protect the non-negotiables.** Even on a broken day, the anchors hold: meals, critical chores, bedtime. The structure bends but does not collapse.
5. **Review at end of day.** What broke the plan? Was it foreseeable? Can you build in a buffer for next time? Was your reaction to the break constructive (replan and continue) or destructive (give up on the whole day)?

**The critical discipline:** A missed morning does not mean a lost day. A lost Monday does not mean a lost week. The person who recovers from a broken schedule and keeps going develops more resilience than the person whose plans never break. Recovery is the skill. The plan breaking is just the training ground.

### Part 7: The Weekly Review — Your Most Important Hour

Once per week — same time each week, non-negotiable — you sit down for a longer review. This is different from the daily five-minute check. This is a thorough assessment of how your week went.

**The weekly review process:**

1. **Pull out your planner.** Look at every day of the past week.
2. **Completion rate.** How many of your planned tasks did you actually complete? Count them. The number matters — not as a grade, but as data. If you planned 40 tasks and completed 25, that is a 62% completion rate. That tells you your plans are too ambitious, your estimates are too optimistic, or your execution needs work.
3. **Time estimate accuracy.** Were your estimates close? Where were the biggest gaps? What consistently takes longer than you think?
4. **Energy patterns.** When were you most productive? When did you drag? Are you scheduling demanding work during your high-energy periods?
5. **Broken schedules.** How many days broke? What caused the breaks? Were they foreseeable?
6. **Plan next week.** Based on what you learned, plan the coming week. Adjust your estimates. Move things around. Account for what you now know about how your time actually works.

**The weekly review is not punishment.** It is calibration. A pilot reviews flight data after every flight — not because they did something wrong, but because the data makes them a better pilot. Your weekly review makes you a better planner, a more accurate estimator, and a more reliable executor. Over months, it is the most powerful tool in this packet.

### Part 8: Time-Blocking — From Intention to Architecture

A to-do list is better than no plan. But a to-do list is still just a list of intentions. Time-blocking converts intentions into architecture.

**The difference:**

To-do list: "Math. History. Piano. Chores."
Time-blocked plan: "9:00-9:45 Math. 9:45-10:00 Break. 10:00-10:30 History reading. 10:30-11:00 History narration. 11:00-11:30 Piano. 11:30-12:00 Chores."

The to-do list hopes things will get done. The time-blocked plan specifies exactly when each thing will happen. Research consistently shows that time-blocked plans are more likely to be executed because they remove the decision of "what should I do now?" The answer is already determined: whatever is in this block.

**Time-blocking principles:**
- Block your most demanding work during your peak energy hours
- Include transition time between blocks (5-10 minutes)
- Block rest and free time — they are real commitments, not just empty gaps
- Build in buffer blocks — 30 minutes of unassigned time that absorbs the inevitable overruns
- Review and adjust your blocks based on what you learn from weekly reviews

**Start simple.** You do not need to block every minute of every day. Begin by blocking your morning — the period from the end of your morning routine to lunch. Get that working reliably. Then extend to the afternoon. Eventually, your entire productive day is time-blocked, with genuine free time in the unblocked spaces.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Daily Planning [Daily, Evening or Morning]
Write tomorrow's plan every evening (or this morning's plan first thing). List every task, estimate the time, sequence the tasks, and time-block them. Execute the plan throughout the day. Review at the end of day. This is the core discipline of IC2.1-B and is practiced every single day.

### Practice 2: Time Estimation Tracking [Daily]
For every task you plan, write your time estimate. After completing the task, record the actual time. Keep a running record. Review accuracy weekly. The goal: within 10% accuracy on routine tasks within three months.

### Practice 3: Weekly Review [Weekly, Same Time Each Week]
Conduct your full weekly review. Completion rate, time estimate accuracy, energy patterns, broken schedules, and next week's plan. Write it down. Keep the reviews so you can see your improvement over months. This practice should take 30-45 minutes.

### Practice 4: The Time Log [One Week Per Month]
For one full week each month, keep a detailed log of how you spend every 30-minute block. At the end of the week, categorize and total the time. Compare where time actually went versus where you think it goes. This is the most honest assessment of your time habits.

### Practice 5: Internal Deadline Setting [Ongoing]
For every multi-day task or project, set a specific internal deadline. Write it in your planner. Work backward to determine the daily work required. Assess honestly when the deadline arrives: did you meet it? Why or why not?

### Practice 6: Schedule Recovery Drill [As Needed]
When your plan breaks — and it will — practice the recovery process. Acknowledge the break, assess what remains, rebuild the remainder, protect non-negotiables, and review at end of day. Over time, your recovery speed will improve until schedule breaks become minor events rather than day-destroying crises.

### Practice 7: Responsibility Handoff [Ongoing, With Mentor]
As you demonstrate reliability, your mentor will transfer planning responsibilities to you that they previously managed. When a responsibility is transferred, it is transferred completely — the mentor stops managing it. You either run it successfully or it does not happen, and the consequence is real. This is how you prove readiness for greater autonomy.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Daily Planning Quality

**Developing well:** The student writes a plan each day that includes specific tasks (not categories), time estimates, a logical sequence, and time-blocks for at least the productive portions of the day. The plan is realistic — it fits inside the available time and accounts for non-negotiables.

**Developing slowly:** Plans are vague ("do schoolwork"), have no time estimates, are wildly unrealistic (8 hours of work in 4 available hours), or are not being written at all. The student is "planning in their head" rather than on paper — which is not planning, it is hoping.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have a physical planner and is it accessible? Have they been taught the six-step process explicitly? Is the plan being written at a consistent time (evening before or first morning)? Has the student been shown what a good daily plan looks like? (Provide examples early on.)

### 2. Time Estimation Accuracy

**Developing well:** The gap between estimated and actual duration is narrowing over weeks. The student can identify their systematic errors ("I always underestimate writing tasks"). Routine tasks are estimated within 10-15% accuracy after 2-3 months.

**Developing slowly:** The student's estimates are not improving. They continue to dramatically underestimate or overestimate without adjusting. They are not recording actual times for comparison.

**Key intervention:** The estimation data must be recorded and reviewed. If the student is only estimating but not timing actuals, there is no feedback loop. Insist on actual time recording for at least the first several months. The calibration cannot happen without the data.

### 3. Deadline Reliability

**Developing well:** The student sets internal deadlines and meets them most of the time. When they miss a deadline, they can articulate why and adjust their process.

**Developing slowly:** Internal deadlines are set but routinely missed without analysis. The student does not notice or does not care that they missed their own deadline.

**Key insight:** Internal deadline discipline is built through consequence and review, not through willpower alone. When an internal deadline is missed, the weekly review should include: what was the deadline? when did you actually finish? what happened? what will you do differently? The analysis is the training. Without it, missed deadlines teach nothing.

### 4. Multiple Demand Management

**Developing well:** The student manages academic work, chores, training, and personal projects within the same day. No single category is consistently abandoned in favor of others. The plan reflects all categories.

**Developing slowly:** The student consistently completes one type of work (usually the one they enjoy) and consistently fails to complete another (usually the one they find boring or difficult). The plan either omits categories or includes them but never executes them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is this a planning problem (the student does not plan for all categories) or an execution problem (the plan includes them but the student avoids them)? A planning problem is an IC2.1 issue — teach better planning. An execution problem may be an IC1.1 issue — the student lacks the discipline to do the hard thing when the easy thing is available.

### 5. Schedule Recovery

**Developing well:** When the plan breaks — an unexpected event, a task taking much longer than estimated, an interruption — the student replans the remainder of the day. They identify what can move, what must stay, and continue productively. The break is a minor event, not a day-ending crisis.

**Developing slowly:** Any disruption to the plan produces one of two reactions: abandoning the plan entirely ("the whole day is ruined") or ignoring the disruption and falling further behind. Neither reaction includes replanning.

**How to train recovery:** When a plan breaks, make it a teaching moment (early on). "Your morning plan broke because math took an hour instead of thirty minutes. You have three hours left. You had five tasks remaining. What stays? What moves? Make a new plan for the afternoon — right now." Over time, this becomes the student's automatic response.

### 6. Weekly Review Quality

**Developing well:** The student conducts a genuine weekly review with specific observations. They can state their completion rate, identify estimation errors, note energy patterns, and articulate what they will change next week. The review produces actionable adjustments.

**Developing slowly:** The review is perfunctory — "it was fine" or rote recitation without insight. The student does not use review data to change next week's plan. The same mistakes repeat without recognition.

**How to improve:** Lead the review with specific questions during Phase 1 and 2. "What was your completion rate this week? Pull out your planner and count." "Show me your three biggest estimation errors. What do they have in common?" "Which day went best? What was different about that day?" The specificity of the questions trains the specificity of the student's self-observation.

### 7. Time-Blocking Adoption

**Developing well:** The student time-blocks their productive hours and can articulate why it helps. Blocks include transition time and buffer time. The student notices when they deviate from the block and returns to the plan.

**Developing slowly:** The student lists tasks but does not assign them to specific time windows. Or they time-block but ignore the blocks during execution, treating them as decorative rather than committal.

### 8. Responsibility Handoff Maintenance

**Developing well:** Responsibilities transferred from mentor to student remain handled reliably. The student does not need the mentor to re-assume management.

**Developing slowly:** Transferred responsibilities decay within days or weeks. The mentor finds themselves re-managing what was supposedly transferred.

**Key response:** If a handoff fails, do not silently re-assume the responsibility. Name it: "I gave you ownership of planning your academic schedule two weeks ago. This week you missed three assignments. That tells me the transfer needs more support. Let's go back to daily plan review for the next two weeks, and then we will try again." The transfer is always the goal. The support is temporary.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Over-Correcting the Plan Before Execution

Your most dangerous instinct as a mentor is to fix the student's plan before they execute it. "You have too much planned for tomorrow — let me help you cut it down." This prevents the student from experiencing the consequence of over-planning. Let the ambitious plan fail. Then review it. The data from failure teaches more than your pre-correction.

Exception: if the plan is so wildly unrealistic that it will produce a genuinely harmful day (no time for meals, no sleep, etc.), intervene on the structural issue, not the planning judgment.

### Accepting "Planning in My Head"

"I have a plan — I just didn't write it down." This is not acceptable. An unwritten plan is not a plan. It is a vague intention. Insist on written plans because: (a) writing forces specificity, (b) written plans can be reviewed for accuracy after the fact, (c) the habit of externalizing plans scales into every professional context, and (d) you cannot review what does not exist.

### Micromanaging Execution

Once the student has a plan, let them execute it. Do not check in every hour. Do not hover. Do not ask "how's it going?" repeatedly. The plan is the commitment. The review is the accountability. In between, the student operates independently. If you micromanage execution, you are running the schedule — the student is just present.

### Confusing IC2.1 with IC1.1 (Again)

This distinction persists at Weathering Storms and becomes more nuanced:

- The student who plans well but does not execute has an **IC1.1 problem** — discipline, will, follow-through.
- The student who executes diligently but plans poorly has an **IC2.1 problem** — estimation, sequencing, time-blocking.
- The student who neither plans nor executes may have both problems — address IC1.1 first, because the best plan in the world is useless without the will to execute it.

### Neglecting the Weekly Review

The weekly review is the most important practice in this packet. It is where learning happens. It is where patterns become visible. It is where adjustments are made. If the weekly review is skipped, shortened, or treated as perfunctory, the entire system loses its feedback loop. Protect the weekly review the way you protect any non-negotiable commitment.

### Allowing Weekend/Vacation Collapse

The planning discipline should persist through weekends and vacations, though the plans themselves will look different (less academic work, more rest, different activities). If the student plans Monday through Friday but abandons all planning on weekends, the habit is only half-formed. Weekend planning may be lighter, but it should exist. "I plan to sleep in until 8:00, then bike ride, then free time until lunch" is a plan. "Whatever happens" is not.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Planning Process
**Strong:** Describes a specific, multi-step process. Names tools. Explains reasoning for sequencing decisions. Shows ownership of the process. "Every evening I open my planner, list tomorrow's tasks, estimate each one, put the hardest stuff in the morning when I'm sharpest, and time-block the whole morning."
**Weak:** "I think about what I need to do." No process, no tools, no specificity.

### Prompt 2: Time Estimation
**Strong:** Can cite specific examples of calibration. "I used to think math took 30 minutes, but after tracking for a month I know it takes 45-50 minutes. Writing always takes longer than I think." Shows data-driven adjustment.
**Weak:** "I'm pretty good at it" (with no evidence) or "I'm bad at it" (with no improvement strategy).

### Prompt 3: Broken Plan
**Strong:** Narrates a specific incident. Describes what broke, what they did, and what they learned. Shows the recovery process in action. "Math took twice as long because of a hard problem set. I cut my afternoon reading in half and moved piano to the next day. I learned to build in buffer time after math."
**Weak:** "Everything went wrong and the day was ruined." No recovery. No analysis.

### Prompt 4: To-Do List vs. Time-Block
**Strong:** Can articulate the functional difference — that a to-do list is intention and a time-blocked plan is architecture. Can explain from experience why the time-blocked plan is more effective.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish the two, or sees time-blocking as unnecessary.

### Prompt 5: Biggest Weakness
**Strong:** Honest, specific, and paired with a strategy. "I consistently overplan my mornings because I am optimistic about how fast I work. I'm building in 15 minutes of buffer time between blocks to fix it." This level of self-awareness is the goal.
**Weak:** "I don't have any weaknesses" (dishonest) or "everything" (unfocused).

### Prompt 6: Weekly Review
**Strong:** Can walk through the review process with specific examples. "Last week my completion rate was 72%, up from 65% the week before. My biggest miss was Wednesday when I underestimated the science project by an hour."
**Weak:** "I look at my planner and see what I did." No specifics, no data, no learning.

### Prompt 7: Non-Negotiables
**Strong:** Can name specific daily anchors that hold regardless — and they are appropriate (sleep, meals, chores, core academic work, training). Shows that the non-negotiables are genuinely non-negotiable, not aspirational.
**Weak:** "I always do everything I plan." (Unrealistic.) Or cannot identify any non-negotiables, suggesting the entire plan is equally flexible.
